title: Palindromic Tree: Count Palindromes by Frequency
description: Count all distinct palindromes with frequency

examples.explanation: String: "aba", length n=3. Initialize pal_count={}. i=0: j=0: substring="a", check palindrome: "a" reversed is "a", so palindrome. pal_count={"a":1}. j=1: substring="ab", check: "ab" reversed "ba", not palindrome. j=2: substring="aba", check: "aba" reversed "aba", palindrome. pal_count={"a":1, "aba":1}. i=1: j=1: substring="b", check: palindrome. pal_count={"a":1, "aba":1, "b":1}. j=2: substring="ba", check: not palindrome. i=2: j=2: substring="a", check: palindrome. Update "a" count: pal_count={"a":2, "aba":1, "b":1}. Return pal_count.
String: "a", n=1. pal_count={}. i=0: j=0: substring="a", check palindrome: yes. pal_count={"a":1}. Return pal_count.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible substrings of the string, check each for palindrome property, and count distinct palindromes with their frequencies using a set or map. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Palindromes with Frequencies via Brute Force
ladders.desc: Generate all substrings, check each for palindrome property, and count distinct palindromes with frequencies using a hash map.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by exhaustively examining every possible substring of the input string. The intuition is that any palindrome must be a contiguous sequence of characters, so by considering all substrings, we guarantee we won't miss any palindrome. We use a hash map (like a dictionary) to store each distinct palindrome as a key and its frequency (count of occurrences) as the value. For each substring, we check if it's a palindrome by comparing characters from both ends inward. If it is, we update the hash map. The brute-force nature makes it simple to understand but inefficient for large strings due to O(n³) time complexity from nested loops and palindrome checks. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty hash map 'pal_count'. 2. Use two nested loops: outer loop 'i' from 0 to n-1 (start index), inner loop 'j' from i to n-1 (end index). 3. For each substring s[i:j+1], check if it's a palindrome by comparing s[i+k] with s[j-k] for k from 0 to (j-i)//2. 4. If palindrome, add or update in 'pal_count': if substring exists as key, increment its value by 1; else, set it to 1. 5. After loops, 'pal_count' contains all distinct palindromes with frequencies. Time: O(n³) because there are O(n²) substrings and each palindrome check takes O(n) in worst case. Space: O(n²) for storing up to O(n²) distinct substrings in the hash map. Connection to L1: This uses the palindrome checking subroutine from L1 for each substring. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle empty substrings (not palindromes by typical definition), off-by-one errors in loop indices, or incorrect palindrome check logic. Real code pattern: def count_palindromes(s): n = len(s); pal_count = {}; for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): substr = s[i:j+1]; if substr == substr[::-1]: pal_count[substr] = pal_count.get(substr, 0) + 1; return pal_count.

examples.trace: String: "aba", length n=3. Initialize pal_count={}. i=0: j=0: substring="a", check palindrome: "a" reversed is "a", so palindrome. pal_count={"a":1}. j=1: substring="ab", check: "ab" reversed "ba", not palindrome. j=2: substring="aba", check: "aba" reversed "aba", palindrome. pal_count={"a":1, "aba":1}. i=1: j=1: substring="b", check: palindrome. pal_count={"a":1, "aba":1, "b":1}. j=2: substring="ba", check: not palindrome. i=2: j=2: substring="a", check: palindrome. Update "a" count: pal_count={"a":2, "aba":1, "b":1}. Return pal_count.
String: "a", n=1. pal_count={}. i=0: j=0: substring="a", check palindrome: yes. pal_count={"a":1}. Return pal_count.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check if a String is a Palindrome
ladders.desc: Determine whether a given string reads the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is crucial because the main problem relies on identifying palindromes among substrings. The intuition is that a palindrome is symmetric around its center, so comparing characters from the start and end moving inward will reveal if it's a palindrome. Step-by-step: 1. Take a string 'str'. 2. Initialize two pointers: 'left' at index 0 and 'right' at len(str)-1. 3. While left < right, compare str[left] and str[right]. If they differ, return False (not a palindrome). 4. Increment left and decrement right. 5. If loop completes without mismatches, return True (palindrome). For empty string, typically not considered a palindrome (return False), but definitions may vary; here we treat it as not a palindrome. Time: O(k) where k is length of string, as we check up to k/2 pairs. Space: O(1) for pointers. Connection to L0: This function is called for each substring in the brute-force approach. Common mistakes: Not handling odd/even lengths correctly (algorithm works for both), off-by-one errors with indices, or forgetting empty string case. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s): return s == s[::-1] (using slicing for simplicity, but pointer method is shown for understanding).

examples.trace: String: "racecar", length=7. left=0 ('r'), right=6 ('r'): equal. left=1 ('a'), right=5 ('a'): equal. left=2 ('c'), right=4 ('c'): equal. left=3 ('e'), right=3: loop ends (left not < right). All comparisons matched, return True.
String: "hello", length=5. left=0 ('h'), right=4 ('o'): not equal, return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generate All Substrings of a String
ladders.desc: Enumerate every contiguous sequence of characters from the input string.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique because palindromes are substrings, so we need to consider all possibilities. The intuition is that a substring is defined by a start index and an end index, so by iterating over all pairs (i, j) where i ≤ j, we can generate all substrings. Step-by-step: 1. Let n be length of string s. 2. Outer loop: i from 0 to n-1 (start index). 3. Inner loop: j from i to n-1 (end index). 4. For each pair (i, j), substring is s[i:j+1] (using slicing). This ensures we cover all contiguous sequences, including single characters and the full string. Time: O(n²) for the nested loops, as there are n*(n+1)/2 substrings. Space: O(1) if just generating indices, but O(n²) if storing all substrings. Connection to L0: This logic is embedded in the brute-force loops to feed substrings to the palindrome checker. Common mistakes: Starting j from 0 instead of i (generates non-contiguous sequences), using j < n instead of j ≤ n-1, or incorrect slicing indices. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): substr = s[i:j+1].

examples.trace: String: "ab", n=2. i=0: j=0: substring="a"; j=1: substring="ab". i=1: j=1: substring="b". Total substrings: ["a", "ab", "b"].
String: "abc", n=3. i=0: j=0:"a"; j=1:"ab"; j=2:"abc". i=1: j=1:"b"; j=2:"bc". i=2: j=2:"c". Substrings: ["a","ab","abc","b","bc","c"].

ladders.title: Building Block — Use a Hash Map to Count Frequencies
ladders.desc: Store distinct items as keys and their counts as values in a dictionary-like structure.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed because we must track how many times each distinct palindrome appears. The intuition is that a hash map allows O(1) average-time lookups and updates, making it efficient to accumulate counts. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty hash map (e.g., dict in Python). 2. For each item (e.g., a palindrome substring), check if it exists as a key in the map. 3. If it does, increment its value by 1. 4. If not, add it as a key with value 1. 5. After processing all items, the map contains frequencies. Time: O(m) for m items, with O(1) per operation on average. Space: O(k) for k distinct items. Connection to L0: This is used to build the final output of distinct palindromes with frequencies. Common mistakes: Not initializing the map, using incorrect key types (e.g., mutable objects), or forgetting to handle missing keys (use get() method or default dict). Real code pattern: freq = {}; for item in items: freq[item] = freq.get(item, 0) + 1.

examples.trace: Items: ['a','b','a','c','b']. Initialize freq={}. Process 'a': not in freq, add {'a':1}. 'b': not in, add {'a':1,'b':1}. 'a': exists, update {'a':2,'b':1}. 'c': not in, add {'a':2,'b':1,'c':1}. 'b': exists, update {'a':2,'b':2,'c':1}. Return freq.
Items: ['x','x','x']. freq={}. 'x': add {'x':1}. 'x': update {'x':2}. 'x': update {'x':3}. Return freq.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over String with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use two loops to traverse all pairs of indices in a string.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used to generate start and end indices for substrings. The intuition is that to explore all contiguous segments, we need to consider every possible starting point and every possible ending point that comes after it. Step-by-step: 1. Let n be length of string s. 2. Outer loop: variable i from 0 to n-1, representing start index. 3. Inner loop: variable j from i to n-1, representing end index. 4. For each i and j, we can access the substring from i to j inclusive. This creates a triangular iteration pattern, covering all (i,j) pairs with i ≤ j. Time: O(n²) due to nested loops. Space: O(1) for loop variables. Connection to L2: This operation is the mechanism to implement substring generation. Common mistakes: Using j from 0 to n-1 (covers non-contiguous pairs), incorrect range boundaries, or not updating j based on i. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(s)): for j in range(i, len(s)): # use i and j.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: A string that reads the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept, essential for understanding the problem. A palindrome is a sequence of characters that is symmetric, meaning it remains unchanged when reversed. Examples include "racecar", "madam", "noon", and single characters like "a". The intuition is that if you compare the first character with the last, the second with the second-last, and so on, all pairs should match. For odd-length strings, the middle character doesn't need a pair. For even-length strings, all characters form pairs. Step-by-step to check: 1. Take a string s. 2. Compare s[0] with s[-1], s[1] with s[-2], etc., until you reach the center. 3. If all comparisons are equal, it's a palindrome; otherwise, it's not. Time: O(n) to check, where n is length. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This definition is the basis for the palindrome-checking subroutine. Common mistakes: Confusing palindromes with other patterns (e.g., anagrams), not considering empty strings (usually not palindromes), or mishandling case sensitivity (in this problem, we assume case-sensitive unless specified). Real code pattern: A simple way is s == s[::-1] in Python.

examples.trace: String: "radar". Compare: index 0 'r' with index 4 'r': equal. Index 1 'a' with index 3 'a': equal. Index 2 'd' with itself (center): no pair needed. All match, so palindrome.
String: "apple". Compare: index 0 'a' with index 4 'e': not equal, so not a palindrome.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use a DP table to precompute palindrome status for all substrings in O(n^2) time, then iterate to count distinct palindromes and their frequencies. This avoids redundant checks by storing intermediate results.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Palindromes with Frequency Using DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP table to count all distinct palindromes and their frequencies in a string.
ladders.explanation: This solution counts all distinct palindromic substrings and their frequencies using dynamic programming. The intuition is to precompute palindrome status for all substrings using a DP table, then use this information to efficiently count palindromes. We create a 2D boolean DP table where dp[i][j] indicates whether substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome. The recurrence relation is: dp[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) AND (j-i <= 2 OR dp[i+1][j-1]). This means a substring is a palindrome if its endpoints match and the inner substring (if length > 2) is also a palindrome. After building the DP table in O(n²) time, we iterate through all substrings again. For each palindrome found, we add it to a dictionary (or set) to track distinct palindromes and increment their frequency. The key insight is that by storing palindrome status in the DP table, we avoid redundant palindrome checks that would occur with naive approaches. Time complexity is O(n²) for building the DP table plus O(n²) for counting, totaling O(n²). Space complexity is O(n²) for the DP table. Common mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors in substring indices, 2) Forgetting to handle single-character palindromes in the base case, 3) Not using memoization properly leading to exponential time. To avoid these, carefully define indices and test with edge cases. This approach is optimal for this specific problem within the O(n²) constraint, though specialized structures like palindromic trees can achieve O(n) for some variations.

examples.trace: String: 'aba', length n=3. Step 1: Initialize DP table 3x3 with False. Step 2: Fill DP table. For i=0 to 2: dp[i][i]=True (single chars are palindromes). For length=2: (0,1): s[0]='a', s[1]='b' not equal → False. (1,2): s[1]='b', s[2]='a' not equal → False. For length=3: (0,2): s[0]='a', s[2]='a' equal AND dp[1][1]=True → True. Step 3: Count palindromes. Initialize dict={}. Iterate all i,j where i≤j: (0,0): substring='a', dp=True → add 'a' with count 1. (0,1): 'ab', dp=False → skip. (0,2): 'aba', dp=True → add 'aba' with count 1. (1,1): 'b', dp=True → add 'b' with count 1. (1,2): 'ba', dp=False → skip. (2,2): 'a', dp=True → increment 'a' to count 2. Final dict: {'a':2, 'b':1, 'aba':1}.

String: 'aab', n=3. Step 1: DP table 3x3. Step 2: Fill DP. Single chars: dp[0][0]=True, dp[1][1]=True, dp[2][2]=True. Length 2: (0,1): s[0]='a', s[1]='a' equal → True. (1,2): s[1]='a', s[2]='b' not equal → False. Length 3: (0,2): s[0]='a', s[2]='b' not equal → False. Step 3: Count. Iterate i,j: (0,0): 'a' → add count 1. (0,1): 'aa' dp=True → add 'aa' count 1. (0,2): 'aab' dp=False → skip. (1,1): 'a' → increment 'a' to 2. (1,2): 'ab' dp=False → skip. (2,2): 'b' → add 'b' count 1. Final: {'a':3, 'aa':1, 'b':1}.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Build Palindrome DP Table
ladders.desc: Construct DP table to store palindrome status for all substrings.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine builds a 2D boolean DP table where dp[i][j] indicates whether substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome. The intuition is to use dynamic programming to avoid redundant checks: instead of checking each substring independently (which would be O(n³)), we store results and build upon smaller substrings. The algorithm: 1) Initialize n x n DP table with False. 2) Mark all single-character substrings as palindromes: dp[i][i] = True for all i. 3) For substrings of length 2 to n: Check if endpoints match (s[i] == s[j]) AND if the inner substring (s[i+1:j]) is a palindrome (dp[i+1][j-1] is True). For length 2 substrings, the inner substring has length 0, so we only need endpoints to match. The recurrence: dp[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) AND (j-i <= 2 OR dp[i+1][j-1]). We fill the table by increasing substring length to ensure dp[i+1][j-1] is computed before dp[i][j]. Time complexity: O(n²) as we iterate over O(n²) substrings, each check is O(1). Space: O(n²) for the table. This feeds into the full problem by providing precomputed palindrome status, enabling efficient counting. Common mistakes: incorrect iteration order (must fill by increasing length), mishandling base cases for length 2. Real code pattern: nested loops: for length in range(2, n+1): for i in range(n-length+1): j = i+length-1; dp[i][j] = (s[i]==s[j]) and (length<=2 or dp[i+1][j-1]).

examples.output: DP table: [[True, False, True], [False, True, False], [False, False, True]]
examples.trace: String 'aba', n=3. Step 1: Initialize 3x3 table all False. Step 2: Single chars: dp[0][0]=True, dp[1][1]=True, dp[2][2]=True. Step 3: Length 2: i=0,j=1: s[0]='a', s[1]='b' not equal → dp[0][1]=False. i=1,j=2: s[1]='b', s[2]='a' not equal → dp[1][2]=False. Step 4: Length 3: i=0,j=2: s[0]='a', s[2]='a' equal AND dp[1][1]=True → dp[0][2]=True. Final table: row0: [T,F,T], row1: [F,T,F], row2: [F,F,T].

String 'aa', n=2. Step 1: Initialize 2x2 False. Step 2: Single chars: dp[0][0]=True, dp[1][1]=True. Step 3: Length 2: i=0,j=1: s[0]='a', s[1]='a' equal → dp[0][1]=True. Final table: row0: [T,T], row1: [F,T].

testCases.expected: [[True, False, False], [False, True, False], [False, False, True]]
[[True, False, True], [False, True, False], [False, False, True]]
[[True, False, False, True], [False, True, True, False], [False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, True]]
[[True, True, True], [False, True, True], [False, False, True]]
[[True, False, False, True], [False, True, True, False], [False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, True]]
[[True, False, False, False], [False, True, False, False], [False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, True]]
[[True, False, False, False, True], [False, True, False, True, False], [False, False, True, False, False], [False, False, False, True, False], [False, False, False, False, True]]

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence for Palindrome Checking
ladders.desc: The recurrence relation that determines if a substring is a palindrome based on endpoints and inner substring.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recurrence relation: dp[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) AND (j-i <= 2 OR dp[i+1][j-1]). This determines whether substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome. Intuition: A string is a palindrome if its first and last characters match AND the substring between them (excluding endpoints) is also a palindrome. For very short strings (length 1 or 2), we only need to check endpoints because there's no inner substring to verify. Specifically: if length=1 (i=j), it's always a palindrome (base case). If length=2 (j-i=1), we only need s[i]==s[j]. If length>=3, we need both s[i]==s[j] AND the inner substring s[i+1:j] to be a palindrome, which is stored in dp[i+1][j-1]. This recurrence allows us to build solutions for larger substrings from smaller ones, avoiding redundant work. Step-by-step: 1) Base: dp[i][i]=True. 2) For length>1, use the recurrence. The condition j-i<=2 handles lengths 1 and 2 efficiently. Time per check is O(1). This is the central technique that enables O(n²) time instead of O(n³). It feeds into building the DP table by providing the rule to fill each cell. Common mistakes: forgetting the OR condition for short strings, incorrect indices for inner substring. Real code: if s[i]==s[j] and (j-i<=2 or dp[i+1][j-1]): dp[i][j]=True. This pattern is key to dynamic programming for palindromes.

examples.trace: Substring is 'aba', indices i=0,j=2. Step 1: Check endpoints: s[0]='a', s[2]='a' → equal. Step 2: Check length: j-i=2, which is <=2, so no need to check inner substring. Step 3: Apply recurrence: dp[0][2] = (s[0]==s[2]) AND (2<=2) → True AND True → True. Result: substring is palindrome.

Substring is 'ab', indices i=0,j=1. Step 1: Check endpoints: s[0]='a', s[1]='b' → not equal. Step 2: Since endpoints not equal, recurrence gives False regardless of length. dp[0][1] = (s[0]==s[1]) AND (1<=2 OR dp[1][0]) → False AND ... → False. Result: not palindrome.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate All Substrings of a String
ladders.desc: Generate all possible substrings of a string efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating over all substrings of a given string. A substring is defined by start index i and end index j where 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n, and n is string length. The intuition is that to analyze all substrings (e.g., for palindrome checking), we need to consider every possible contiguous sequence. The total number of substrings is n(n+1)/2, which is O(n²). The algorithm uses nested loops: outer loop for start index i from 0 to n-1, inner loop for end index j from i to n-1. This ensures each substring s[i:j+1] is visited exactly once. Step-by-step: 1) Get string length n. 2) For i in range(n): For j in range(i, n): process substring s[i:j+1]. Time complexity: O(n²) for the loops, with O(1) work per substring if just accessing indices. Space: O(1) extra. This feeds into the core logic by providing the substrings to check using the DP recurrence. It's also used in the counting phase of the full problem. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in slice indices (remember j is inclusive), inefficient generation using three loops. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): substr = s[i:j+1]. This is fundamental for many string problems involving substrings.

examples.trace: String 'abc', n=3. Step 1: i=0: j=0 → substring s[0:1]='a'. j=1 → s[0:2]='ab'. j=2 → s[0:3]='abc'. Step 2: i=1: j=1 → s[1:2]='b'. j=2 → s[1:3]='bc'. Step 3: i=2: j=2 → s[2:3]='c'. Total 6 substrings.
String 'aa', n=2. Step 1: i=0: j=0 → 'a'. j=1 → 'aa'. Step 2: i=1: j=1 → 'a'. Total 3 substrings.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check if Two Characters Are Equal
ladders.desc: Compare two characters from a string for equality.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two characters at given indices in a string to see if they are equal. Intuition: In palindrome checking, we need to compare the first and last characters of a substring; if they match, it's a candidate for being a palindrome. The operation is simple: access the characters at indices i and j in string s, then check if s[i] == s[j]. In programming, strings are typically indexed starting from 0. Step-by-step: 1) Ensure indices are within bounds: 0 ≤ i,j < length of string. 2) Access s[i] and s[j] using array indexing. 3) Use equality operator (==) to compare. Time complexity: O(1) as it's a constant-time access and comparison. Space: O(1). This feeds into the building block by being part of substring processing and is crucial for the DP recurrence where we check endpoint equality. Common mistakes: comparing characters incorrectly (e.g., case sensitivity), index out of bounds errors. Real code pattern: if s[i] == s[j]: do something. This is a fundamental operation in string manipulation.

examples.trace: String 'hello'. Step 1: Access s[0] = 'h'. Step 2: Access s[1] = 'e'. Step 3: Compare 'h' == 'e' → False.
String 'racecar', length 7. Step 1: Access s[0] = 'r'. Step 2: Access s[6] = 'r'. Step 3: Compare 'r' == 'r' → True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition and examples of palindromes.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence (such as a string of characters) that reads the same forward and backward. Intuition: It's symmetric around its center. For example, 'racecar' reversed is 'racecar', so it's a palindrome. Key properties: 1) Single characters are always palindromes (e.g., 'a'). 2) Empty string is often considered a palindrome, but in this problem, we focus on non-empty substrings. 3) Palindromes can be of any length: short like 'aa' or long like 'madam'. To check if a string is a palindrome manually: write the string and its reverse, then compare if they are identical. Algorithmically: compare characters from start and end moving inward. Step-by-step for string s of length n: 1) Initialize left=0, right=n-1. 2) While left < right: if s[left] != s[right], return False; else increment left and decrement right. 3) If loop completes, return True. Time: O(n) for checking one string. This foundational concept is prerequisite for all higher levels, as the problem revolves around identifying palindromes. Common mistakes: forgetting that single characters are palindromes, mishandling even vs odd lengths. Real code: def is_palindrome(s): return s == s[::-1] in Python. This concept is essential for string algorithms.

examples.trace: String 'radar'. Step 1: Reverse is 'radar'. Step 2: Compare original and reverse: 'radar' == 'radar' → True. Alternatively, compare characters: s[0]='r', s[4]='r' equal; s[1]='a', s[3]='a' equal; s[2]='d' is middle. All match, so palindrome.
String 'hello'. Step 1: Reverse is 'olleh'. Step 2: Compare: 'hello' != 'olleh' → False. Alternatively, compare characters: s[0]='h', s[4]='o' not equal → immediately False.

approaches.name: Palindromic Tree (Eertree) O(n)
approaches.summary: Construct a Palindromic Tree (Eertree) that efficiently represents all distinct palindromic substrings in linear time, then traverse the tree to count frequencies. This is the most optimal approach for this problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count All Distinct Palindromes with Frequency Using Palindromic Tree
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Palindromic Tree (Eertree) to count all distinct palindromic substrings and their frequencies in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: The Palindromic Tree (Eertree) is a specialized data structure that efficiently represents all distinct palindromic substrings of a string in linear time. It consists of nodes representing palindromes, with edges representing character extensions. Each node stores the palindrome length, frequency count, and a suffix link to the longest proper palindromic suffix. The algorithm processes the string character by character, maintaining a current node representing the longest palindromic suffix ending at the current position. For each new character, it traverses suffix links to find the longest palindrome where adding the character at both ends forms a new palindrome. If this palindrome doesn't exist, a new node is created. The frequency of each palindrome is incremented when it's found, and at the end, frequencies are propagated through suffix links to count all occurrences. The intuition is that palindromes have symmetric properties that allow efficient extension checking via suffix links, avoiding redundant comparisons. Time complexity is O(n) because each character triggers at most O(1) suffix link traversals (amortized), and space is O(n) for storing nodes. This feeds into the problem by providing the complete solution. Common mistakes include incorrect suffix link updates, mishandling of odd/even length roots, and forgetting to propagate frequencies. Real code pattern involves initializing two root nodes (length -1 and 0), processing each character with get_link function to find appropriate node, and updating structures.

examples.trace: Initialize tree with roots: node 0 (len -1, suffix link to self), node 1 (len 0, suffix link to node 0). Process 'a': current = 1, get_link(1,'a') traverses suffix links: from node 1 to node 0, check if adding 'a' to node 0 forms palindrome? Node 0 represents empty string, adding 'a' gives 'a', which doesn't exist, create node 2 (len 1, suffix link to node 1). Update current = 2, increment count[2] = 1. Process 'b': current = 2, get_link(2,'b') from node 2 (palindrome 'a') suffix link to node 1, check adding 'b' to node 1 gives 'b', doesn't exist, create node 3 (len 1, suffix link to node 1). current = 3, count[3] = 1. Process 'a': current = 3, get_link(3,'a') from node 3 suffix link to node 1, check adding 'a' to node 1 gives 'a', exists as node 2, set current = 2, count[2]++ = 2. Process 'b': current = 2, get_link(2,'b') from node 2 suffix link to node 1, check adding 'b' to node 1 gives 'b', exists as node 3, current = 3, count[3]++ = 2. Process 'a': current = 3, get_link(3,'a') from node 3 suffix link to node 1, check adding 'a' to node 1 gives 'a', exists as node 2, current = 2, count[2]++ = 3. After processing, propagate frequencies: start from last node, add its count to suffix link node. Node 2 count 3 added to node 1 (now 3), node 3 count 2 added to node 1 (now 5), etc. Extract distinct palindromes: node 2 = 'a' freq 3, node 3 = 'b' freq 2, node 4 (created during 'aba') = 'aba' freq 2, node 5 = 'bab' freq 1, node 6 = 'ababa' freq 1.

Initialize tree. Process 'a': current = 1, get_link(1,'a') finds no existing node for 'a', create node 2 (len 1), current = 2, count[2] = 1. Process 'a': current = 2, get_link(2,'a') from node 2 suffix link to node 1, check adding 'a' to node 1 gives 'a', exists as node 2, so current = 2, count[2]++ = 2. Also, during processing, when adding second 'a', check if can extend palindrome 'a' to 'aa': from current node 2, check if previous character (position i-len[2]-1) is 'a', yes, so create node 3 for 'aa' (len 2), set suffix link appropriately. Propagate frequencies: node 2 count 2 added to node 1, node 3 count 1 added to its suffix link. Result: 'a' freq 2, 'aa' freq 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — get_link Function for Finding Palindrome Extension
ladders.desc: Implement the helper function that finds the longest palindrome suffix where adding a new character creates a new palindrome.
ladders.explanation: The get_link function is the core mechanism in Palindromic Tree that determines where to extend or create new palindrome nodes. Given a current node representing the longest palindromic suffix ending at position i-1, and a new character c at position i, it finds the longest palindrome such that adding c to both ends forms a valid palindrome. This is done by traversing suffix links starting from the current node. The suffix link of a node points to the longest proper palindromic suffix of that palindrome. The function repeatedly follows suffix links until it finds a node where the character before the palindrome (at position i - len[node] - 1) matches c, meaning extending that palindrome with c on both sides is valid. If such a node exists, it returns that node's child via edge c if it exists, or indicates a new palindrome needs creation. The intuition is that suffix links efficiently navigate through decreasing palindrome lengths without rechecking characters, leveraging the symmetric property of palindromes. Time complexity per call is amortized O(1) because total traversals are bounded by O(n). Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each character to update the tree. Common mistakes include infinite loops if suffix links are incorrect, not handling the imaginary root properly, and off-by-one errors in index calculations. Real code pattern: function get_link(node, c, s, i): while True: check if s[i - len[node] - 1] == c, if yes break; else node = suffix_link[node]; then return node or create new node.

examples.input: Current node = 2 (palindrome 'a'), character 'b', string 'ab', position i=1
examples.output: Node 1 (empty palindrome) after traversal, indicating new palindrome 'b' can be created
examples.trace: Start with node 2 (len 1). Check if s[i - len[2] - 1] = s[1 - 1 - 1] = s[-1] (invalid, since i=1, len[2]=1, i-len[2]-1 = -1). So, follow suffix link of node 2 to node 1 (len 0). Now check s[i - len[1] - 1] = s[1 - 0 - 1] = s[0] = 'a', which does not match 'b'. Follow suffix link of node 1 to node 0 (len -1). For node 0, len is -1, so condition i - len[0] - 1 = 1 - (-1) - 1 = 1, check s[1] (but i is current position, need to check previous char? Actually, for node 0, it represents imaginary root, so we consider it matches trivially. In implementation, node 0 handles base case. So return node 1 as the point to check for edge 'b'. Since edge from node 1 with 'b' doesn't exist, it signals to create new node for 'b'.

examples.input: Current node = 3 (palindrome 'b'), character 'a', string 'aba', position i=2
examples.output: Node 2 (palindrome 'a') after traversal, indicating palindrome 'a' exists and can be extended
examples.trace: Start with node 3 (len 1). Check s[i - len[3] - 1] = s[2 - 1 - 1] = s[0] = 'a', matches 'a'? Yes, because we're checking if adding 'a' to both ends of palindrome 'b' gives 'aba', which requires previous char 'a'. It matches, so return the child of node 3 via edge 'a' if exists. In this case, from node 3, edge 'a' might not exist, but in context, we might be checking from proper node. Actually, in standard algorithm, we use get_link to find the node to extend from. Here, since match found at node 3 itself, we would check if edge 'a' from node 3 exists. If not, we create new node. But typically, get_link returns the node after traversal where match is found. So output is node 3 itself, indicating to check edge from node 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Suffix Links and Palindrome Extension in Eertree
ladders.desc: Understand how suffix links enable efficient palindrome discovery and extension in linear time.
ladders.explanation: Suffix links are the key innovation in Palindromic Tree that make it O(n). Each node (except roots) has a suffix link pointing to the longest proper palindromic suffix of that palindrome. For example, for palindrome 'ababa', its longest proper palindromic suffix is 'aba', so its suffix link points to the node representing 'aba'. This creates a chain of decreasing palindrome lengths. When processing a new character, we use the current node (longest palindrome ending at previous position) and traverse suffix links to find the longest palindrome where adding the new character at both ends is valid. This traversal works because if a palindrome doesn't extend with the new character, its suffix link points to a shorter palindrome that might extend, and due to palindrome symmetry, we skip redundant checks. The intuition is that palindromes are nested: any palindrome's suffixes are also palindromes, so suffix links navigate this hierarchy efficiently. Time complexity is O(1) amortized per character because each suffix link traversal moves to a strictly shorter palindrome, and total traversals are bounded by O(n). Space is O(n) for storing links. This feeds into L1 by providing the logic for get_link. Common mistakes include setting suffix links incorrectly (e.g., for new nodes, suffix link should point to the palindrome found via get_link on its suffix), or not handling the two root nodes properly (length -1 and 0). Real code pattern: when creating new node for palindrome of length L, set its suffix link to the node returned by get_link on the suffix link of its parent.

examples.output: Nodes: 2('a') suffix link to 1, 3('b') suffix link to 1, 4('aba') suffix link to 2
examples.trace: Process 'a': create node 2 for 'a', its longest proper palindromic suffix is empty string (node 1), so suffix_link[2] = 1. Process 'b': create node 3 for 'b', suffix_link[3] = 1. Process 'a': current node is 3, get_link finds that adding 'a' to empty string gives 'a', which exists as node 2, so set current = 2. Also, check if can extend 'b' to 'aba'? From node 3, check previous char s[2 - len[3] - 1] = s[0] = 'a', matches 'a', so create node 4 for 'aba'. To set suffix_link[4], find longest proper palindromic suffix of 'aba', which is 'a' (node 2), so suffix_link[4] = 2.

examples.output: Nodes: 2('a') suffix link to 1, 3('aa') suffix link to 2
examples.trace: Process first 'a': create node 2 for 'a', suffix_link[2] = 1. Process second 'a': current node is 2, get_link finds that adding 'a' to empty string gives 'a', exists as node 2, so current = 2. Also, extend 'a' to 'aa': from node 2, check previous char s[1 - len[2] - 1] = s[-1] (invalid), so follow suffix link to node 1, then check s[1 - len[1] - 1] = s[0] = 'a', matches, so create node 3 for 'aa'. Its longest proper palindromic suffix is 'a' (node 2), so suffix_link[3] = 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing Palindromes as Nodes in a Tree
ladders.desc: Learn how to structure palindrome information in nodes with length, edges, and suffix links.
ladders.explanation: In Palindromic Tree, each distinct palindrome is represented as a node. Each node contains: length of the palindrome, a dictionary of edges to child nodes (where an edge labeled with character c leads to the palindrome formed by adding c to both ends of the current palindrome), a suffix link pointing to the longest proper palindromic suffix, and a frequency count. The tree has two special root nodes: node 0 with length -1 (imaginary root for odd-length palindromes) and node 1 with length 0 (empty string for even-length palindromes). These roots handle base cases when traversing suffix links. The intuition is that palindromes can be built incrementally by extending shorter palindromes symmetrically, and the tree structure captures all possible extensions efficiently. Time complexity for operations per node is O(1) for access, and space is O(n) due to storing nodes for up to n distinct palindromes. This feeds into L2 by providing the data structure for suffix links. Common mistakes include not initializing roots correctly, confusing odd/even roots, or inefficient edge storage. Real code pattern: class Node: length, edges dict, suffix_link, count.

examples.trace: Initialize nodes: node 0: length -1, edges empty, suffix_link to self; node 1: length 0, edges empty, suffix_link to node 0. After processing 'a', create node 2: length 1, edges empty, suffix_link to node 1. After processing 'b', create node 3: length 1, edges empty, suffix_link to node 1. Tree structure: from node 1, edge 'a' to node 2, edge 'b' to node 3.

Initialize as above. After first 'a', node 2 as before. After second 'a', from node 2, since extending 'a' to 'aa' is valid, create node 3: length 2, edges empty, suffix_link to node 2. Also, update edges: node 2 gets edge 'a' to node 3.

testCases.expected: 4 nodes: roots, plus nodes for 'a' and 'b'
12 nodes: roots plus each distinct palindrome

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Palindrome Extension with Character Match
ladders.desc: Determine if a given palindrome can be extended by adding a specific character to both ends.
ladders.explanation: To extend a palindrome by adding a character c to both ends, we need to check if the character immediately before the palindrome in the string matches c. For a palindrome of length L ending at position i-1, the character at position i-L-1 must equal c. This ensures symmetry: the new palindrome will be c + palindrome + c. This operation is fundamental because it validates whether a new palindrome can be formed without explicitly checking all characters. The intuition is that palindromes are symmetric, so extending requires matching the outer characters. Time complexity is O(1) per check, and space is O(1). This feeds into L3 by being used when creating new nodes in the tree. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in index calculation, not handling edge cases when i-L-1 is negative (which indicates no extension possible, leading to suffix link traversal), or confusing string indices. Real code pattern: if i - length[node] - 1 >= 0 and s[i - length[node] - 1] == c, then extension is valid.

examples.trace: Palindrome 'a' ends at position 0. To extend to 'aba' by adding 'b', need to check character at position i - length - 1 = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1. Since -1 is invalid index, extension is not possible directly. This triggers suffix link traversal in the full algorithm.

Palindrome 'a' ends at position 0. Check position i - length - 1 = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1, invalid, so extension not possible directly. But in context, when i=1, we are processing second 'a', and from current node for 'a', we check previous char, which is s[0] = 'a', matches 'a', so actually extension is valid? Wait, careful: For palindrome 'a' to extend to 'aa', we need to add 'a' to both ends, so the character before 'a' in the string must be 'a'. Since 'a' is at position 0, the character before it is position -1, which doesn't exist, so technically extension fails. However, in Eertree, we use suffix links to handle this: we traverse to empty palindrome and then extend. So this basic operation returns False, but the algorithm uses it as part of larger logic.

testCases.expected: True (empty string can always extend if previous char matches?) Actually, for length 0, i - 0 - 1 = -1, invalid, so False, but in algorithm, empty string is handled via roots.
Check: i=2, length=2, i-length-1=-1, invalid, False
i=4, length=3, i-length-1=0, s[0]='a', matches 'a', True
i=2, length=1, i-length-1=0, s[0]='a', does not match 'c', False
i=6, length=4, i-length-1=1, s[1]='a', does not match 'r', False
i=6, length=5, i-length-1=0, s[0]='r', does not match 'e', False

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Palindrome Properties and Suffix Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what a palindrome is and how suffixes of palindromes relate to each other.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards, e.g., 'racecar'. Key properties: symmetry around the center, and any suffix of a palindrome is also a palindrome if it starts at a symmetric position. For example, in 'ababa', suffixes include 'aba', 'ba', 'a', etc., but only 'aba' and 'a' are palindromes because they are symmetric parts. The longest proper palindromic suffix of a palindrome is the longest palindrome that is a suffix and not the entire string. For 'ababa', its suffixes are 'baba', 'aba', 'ba', 'a'; among these, 'aba' and 'a' are palindromes, so the longest is 'aba'. This concept is foundational because Palindromic Tree uses suffix links to navigate these relationships efficiently. The intuition is that palindromes can be decomposed into smaller palindromes, enabling incremental construction. Time and space are not applicable here as it's conceptual. This feeds into L4 by explaining why extension checks work. Common mistakes include confusing suffixes with substrings or not identifying proper suffixes. Real code pattern: conceptual understanding only.

examples.output: Suffixes: 'acecar', 'cecar', 'ecar', 'car', 'ar', 'r'. Palindromic suffixes: 'r', 'e', 'cec', etc. Longest proper palindromic suffix: 'e' (since 'racecar' length 7, suffixes of length <7: 'acecar' not palindrome, 'cecar' not, 'ecar' not, 'car' not, 'ar' not, 'r' is palindrome but shorter than 'e'? Actually, 'e' is not a suffix; 'e' is a single character in middle. Wait, correct: suffixes are from end: 'racecar' -> remove first char: 'acecar' (not palindrome), remove first two: 'cecar' (not), etc. The palindromic suffixes are 'r' (length 1) and maybe others? 'e' is not a suffix. So longest is 'r'.
Suffixes: 'a', ''. Palindromic suffixes: 'a' and '' (empty string). Longest proper palindromic suffix: 'a' (since empty string is proper but shorter).

Palindrome 'aba', find longest proper palindromic suffix
Palindrome 'abba', find longest proper palindromic suffix
